content.explanation:
📊 What Tables Are Actually For

Tables exist for ONE specific job: displaying genuinely tabular data — information that naturally has rows and columns, like a price list, a class timetable, or a sports league table. A common beginner mistake (very common in older websites) is using tables to control general PAGE LAYOUT — putting a whole page's header, sidebar, and footer into table cells. That practice is outdated and considered bad HTML today, because layout is CSS's job, and screen readers get confused trying to read a layout table as if it were real data. Use tables only when your content genuinely IS rows and columns of related data.

🗓️ The Timetable Analogy

Picture a school timetable taped to a wall: down the side are the days of the week, across the top are time slots, and in each cell is a specific subject. That grid structure — row, column, cell — is exactly what HTML tables represent directly in code.

🧱 Three-Layer Structure

A table is built in layers, much like the container-plus-items pattern from lists, but one level deeper:

  • Table — the outer container for the whole grid
  • Row — one horizontal line across the table
  • Cell — one individual box within a row, containing the actual data

You always work from the outside in: define the table, then define each row, then define each cell within that row.
content.syntax_breakdown:
🧱 Basic Table Structure

<table>
  <tr>
    <th>Day</th>
    <th>Subject</th>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>Monday</td>
    <td>Math</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>Tuesday</td>
    <td>Science</td>
  </tr>
</table>

Breaking down each tag:

  • <table> — the outer container for the entire grid
  • <tr> — short for 'table row.' Each <tr> creates one horizontal line
  • <th> — short for 'table header.' Used for column titles (like 'Day' and 'Subject' here) — browsers automatically bold and center this text by default, AND screen readers announce it specially as a header, not just regular data
  • <td> — short for 'table data.' This is a normal, regular cell holding actual information

Notice the nesting depth here: <table> contains <tr> elements, and each <tr> contains <th> or <td> elements. This is three levels deep — table, then row, then cell — and getting the nesting order right is essential, because a <td> placed directly inside a <table> without a <tr> wrapper is invalid.

🆚 th vs td — Why the Distinction Matters

It's tempting to just use <td> everywhere since it's easier to remember, but using <th> correctly for header cells gives meaning, not just bold styling — it tells screen readers and search engines 'this cell is a LABEL for the column/row below/beside it,' which is genuinely useful structural information, not decoration.

📐 How Many Cells Per Row?

Every <tr> in a table should generally have the SAME number of cells, so columns line up correctly. If one row has 2 cells and another has 3, your table grid will look broken and misaligned.
content.common_mistakes:
⚠️ Common Beginner Mistakes

  • Using tables for page layout instead of genuine tabular data — outdated practice, breaks accessibility, and fights against responsive design.
  • Using for header cells instead of , losing meaningful structure.
  • Inconsistent cell counts between rows, causing a visually broken/misaligned grid.
  • Forgetting the wrapper and placing directly inside .

mini_challenge.instruction:
🎯 Challenge:

1. Build a table with a header row containing 'Day' and 'Activity'
2. Add two data rows with real days and activities of your choice
3. Below the table, add a paragraph that says: tables organize rows and columns
4. Run and verify your code
mini_challenge.hint:
👉 Example:
Day Activity
Monday Coding practice

tables organize rows and columns

quiz_engine.question: What is the correct nesting order for building a table?
quiz_engine.explanation_feedback: Correct 🎉 You work from the outside in: table is the outer container, tr defines each row, and td/th are the individual cells inside each row.

takeaways: Tables are for genuine tabular data only, not general page layout
Structure flows outside-in: table > tr (row) > td or th (cell)
<th> is for header cells — bolded by default and announced specially to screen readers
Keep the same number of cells in every row to avoid a broken grid

cheat_sheet.<table></table>: Outer container for the whole grid
cheat_sheet.<th></th>: Header cell — bold, announced as a label
